title: 9th Circuit Lifts Restrictions on ICE Crowd Control Weapons at Portland Facility
excerpt: A federal appeals court ruled 2-1 that protesters had not shown an 'unwritten policy' of retaliation by federal agents at the Portland ICE facility, lifting lower-court restrictions on tear gas and crowd control weapons. The ruling was a setback for protesters who argued agents used indiscriminate force.
content:
On April 27, 2026, a three-judge panel of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 9th Circuit ruled that federal officers could continue to use crowd control weapons outside the ICE facility in Portland's South Waterfront neighborhood without the restrictions imposed by two lower federal court judges.

In a separate ruling on the same day, the court found 2-1 that plaintiffs — protesters and independent journalists — had not demonstrated an 'unwritten policy' of retaliation, even though a district court had previously found officers knew their deployments of toxic clouds harmed apartment tenants' health.

  • The lower court rulings had found that federal officers deployed tear gas, pepper balls, and flash-bangs against nonviolent protesters and that the chemical munitions seeped into a neighboring affordable housing complex, Gray's Landing.
  • The majority opinion stated: 'While some individual incidents might indicate an arguably disproportionate use of force, they alone do not amount to an unwritten policy of retaliation.'
  • The appeals court also found that the district judge had improperly granted class certification, limiting the scope of who was protected by the initial order.
  • Attorneys for Gray's Landing tenants noted that Judge Amy Baggio's initial ruling had found federal officers 'knew the toxic clouds harmed tenants' health but deployed them anyway.'
